coredata.dc:title: Novel magnetic fluorescence probe based on carbon quantum dots-doped molecularly imprinted polymer for AHLs signaling molecules sensing in fish juice and milk
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Zhimeng
Li, Zhuanying
Jin, Yuting
Ren, Taotao
Chen, Jin'ai
Wang, Xianghong
Zhong, Keli
Tang, Lijun
Tang, Yiwei
Cao, Meirong
coredata.dc:description: A magnetic fluorescence probe was fabricated by coating carbon quantum dots-doped molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) layers on the surface of Fe3O4 particles (MFMP) for detection of N-acyl homoserine lactones (AHLs) signaling molecules. N-Z-L-homoserine lactone molecular was used as the template to prepare AHLs MIP layers, employing MAA and HEMA as functional monomers. The developed MFMP owned superparamagnetism, fluorescence, fast response and class-selectivity. If AHLs (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL) were captured by the MFMP, they quenched the fluorescence of the probe. Fluorescence dropped linearly in the concentration ranges of 3.65 × 10−3 μmol/L–0.96 × 10−1 μmol/L for AHLs. The MFMP was applied to the analysis of fish juice and milk samples, and recoveries ranged from 83.10% to 90.74% with relative standard deviation less than 5.1%. This study offered a novel strategy to fabricated AHLs fluorescence probe with great potential for wide-ranging application in agri-food products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-acyl homoserine lactones
Carbon quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Magnetic fluorescence probe
Quorum sensing
Signaling molecules

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of magnetic fluorescent MIPs probe (MFMP)
Extraction of AHLs from bacterial culture medium
Preparation of food samples and extraction of AHLs
Determination of AHLs in bacterial suspensions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FI-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, CDs and MFMP (A). Magnetization curves of Fe3O4 and MFMP (B). XRD spectra of SiO2, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and MFMP (C). TEM of CDs (D), Fe3O4 (E) and MFMP (F).
The static adsorption of MFMP and MFNP (A). Adsorption kinetics of MFMP and pseudo-second-order kinetic analysis plot of the template onto the MFMP (B).
The fluorescence spectra of MFMP incubated with different concentrations of C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL signaling molecules.
Schematic of the synthesis and recognition of MFMP.
Stern-Volmer equations with correlation coefficient (R 2), linear range, LOD and RSD (n = 6).
Determination of AHLs in fish juice and milk samples by MFMP-based fluorescence assay and LC–MS (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Novel magnetic fluorescence probe based on carbon quantum dots-doped molecularly imprinted polymer for AHLs signaling molecules sensing in fish juice and milk

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhimeng Cui and Zhuanying Li contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A magnetic fluorescence probe was fabricated by coating carbon quantum dots-doped molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) layers on the surface of Fe3O4 particles (MFMP) for detection of N-acyl homoserine lactones (AHLs) signaling molecules. N-Z-L-homoserine lactone molecular was used as the template to prepare AHLs MIP layers, employing MAA and HEMA as functional monomers. The developed MFMP owned superparamagnetism, fluorescence, fast response and class-selectivity. If AHLs (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL) were captured by the MFMP, they quenched the fluorescence of the probe. Fluorescence dropped linearly in the concentration ranges of 3.65 × 10−3 μmol/L–0.96 × 10−1 μmol/L for AHLs. The MFMP was applied to the analysis of fish juice and milk samples, and recoveries ranged from 83.10% to 90.74% with relative standard deviation less than 5.1%. This study offered a novel strategy to fabricated AHLs fluorescence probe with great potential for wide-ranging application in agri-food products.

ce:section.ce:para: N-acyl homoserine lactone (AHLs) is a kind of signaling molecules in quorum sensing of Gram-negative bacteria, by which bacterial cell can sense their population density and up-regulate specific phenotypes including the virulence factor production, food spoilage and biofilm formation (Fuqua, Winans, & Greenberg, 1994; Ren, Ma, Liu, Zhuang, & Zhuang, 2016; Zhao, Zhu, Ye, Ge, & Li, 2016; Huang et al., 2016). Lots of foodborne pathogens like Aeromonas hydrophila, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Hafnia alvei are capable of producing AHLs signaling molecules during their growth period (Bruhn et al., 2004; Jahid, Mizan, Ha, & Ha, 2015; de Dieu Habimana et al., 2018; Sharma, Gopu, Sivasankar, & Shetty, 2019). These signaling molecules play a significant role in food spoilage and foodborne diseases (Bruhn et al., 2004; Wynn et al., 2018). Therefore, monitoring AHLs signaling molecules as a universal biomarker of Gram-negative bacteria in food samples can help us to identify the trends of the contaminated and unsafe food, preventing large scale foodborne outbreaks.

Several methods for AHLs detection have been developed, including high-performance liquid chromatography (Fekete et al., 2007), liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) (Patel, Moore, Blackwell, & Amador-Noguez, 2016; May, Eisenhauer, Coulston, & Campagna, 2012; Ortori et al., 2011), gas spectrometry-mass spectrometry (Cataldi, Bianco, Palazzo, & Quaranta, 2007) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Pearman, Lawrence-Snyder, Angel, & Decho, 2007). These instrumentation-based methods are useful and popular analytical approaches with high sensitivity, but the expensive equipment and tedious sample pretreatment limited their practical and widespread use (de Dieu Habimana et al., 2018; Tang et al., 2017). Moreover, fluorescent probe (de Dieu Habimana et al., 2018; Wynn et al., 2018; Charlesworth, Kimyon, Manefield, & Burns, 2015; Deng, Zhuang, Ma, Yu, & Zhuang, 2014) and electrochemical sensor (Jiang et al., 2017, 2018; Jiang, Jiang, Shao, & Sun, 2016) were also fabricated for development of AHLs analytical assay.

Fluorescence probe had gained an ever-growing attention due to its sensitive, fast and reproducible optical signal, and had been widely applied for food safety and environmental monitoring (Zhang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2017). As a new artificial receptor for optical probe and sensor technology, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) earned a good reputation owing to their high affinity and selectivity, low cost, easy preparation and stability (Pan, Chen, Ma, & Pan, 2018; Ahmad, Bedwell, Esen, Garcia-Cruz, & Piletsky, 2019). MIPs-based fluorescence probe was firstly prepared for AHLs detection using 2,5-dimethl-4-hydroxy-3(2H)-furanone as analog template, methacrylic acid as functional monomer, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as cross-linker and semiconductor quantum dots CdSe/ZnS as signal transduction element (de Dieu Habimana et al., 2018). Semiconductor quantum dots such as CdSe/ZnS, CdTe and CdS owning good photostability, low background noise and broad excitation are good fluorophores, but their intrinsic toxicity to environment and health has caused extensive concern (Wang et al., 2015; Li, Wang, Chen, & Liu, 2019). Therefore, a further investigation of using eco-friendly quantum dots as the resource of fluorescent signal to fabricate MIPs probe for AHLs monitoring is of high necessity.

Multifunctional hybrids combining with unique magnetic and fluorescent properties are expected to achieve in one probe that can improve the sensitivity and feasibility for analytes in complex sample matrix (Chen et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2018). To achieve this aim, Wei and co-workers developed a multifunctional fluorescence probe for determination of 2,4,6-trichlorophenol using Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the core that was coated with a MIPs doped with Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots (Wei et al., 2018). Using the “layer-by-layer” assembly of MIPs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the up-conversion particles, Tang and co-worker fabricated a multifunctional MIPs fluorescence probe for sensing quinolones (Tang et al., 2018). However, little work has been done to monitor QS signaling molecules using a single MIPs-based magnetic fluorescence probe. Consequently, it is of significance to develop a multifunctional probe for the determination of trace level of AHLs signaling molecules in food samples.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) is an environmentally and biologically luminescent nanomaterial, owning strong resistance to photobleaching, high photochemical stability and facile synthesis process (Sharma et al., 2019). Importantly, CDs had been synthesized by green synthesis methods (e.g., microwave method and hydrothermal method) using natural resources (e.g., citric acid, candle soot and glycine) (Barati, Shamsipur, Arkan, Hosseinzadeh, & Abdollahi, 2015; Chen et al., 2013). In this work, citric acid and glycine-based CDs was prepared by hydrothermal method, and a multifunctional magnetic fluorescence probe was fabricated by coating CDs-doped MIPs layers on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. Here, Fe3O4 particles was used as supports with magnetic performance, MIPs layers provided specific recognition and binding sites for the AHLs molecules, and the doped CDs indicated the selective binding events by emitting fluorescence signal. As a novel sensing fluorescence probe with excellent magnetic property and high sensitivity, it was also used to quantify AHLs in real food samples.

N-Z-L-homoserine lactone (N-Z-L-HSL), FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, citric acid, glycine, methacrylic acid (MAA), 2-hydroxyethylmethacrylate (HEMA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) and γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxy-silane (MPS) were supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai, China, https://www.aladdin-e.com). N-butyryl-L-homoserine lactone (C4-HSL), N-hexanoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C6-HSL), N-octanoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C8-HSL), N-decanoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C10-HSL), N-dodecanoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C12-HSL) and N-myristoyl-L-homoserine lactone (C14-HSL) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China, https://www.sigmaaldrich.com/china-mainland.html). Acetonitrile, ammonia, methanol, ethanol, acetic acid, isopropanol and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, https://shreagent.lookchem.com). All chemical and reagents used in this work were of the highest available purity and at least of analytical grade.

The size and morphology of samples were examined using Tecnai G20 transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement were carried out using a UItima IV X-ray power diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) over the 2θ range of 10-80°. Magnetization was measured by a Quantum Design MPMS-XL 5 superconducting quantum interference device (Quantum Design, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (4000–400 cm−1) was recorded on a Vector 22 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, German). Fluorescence analysis was performed using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 1 × 1 cm quartz cell.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared using a facile hydrothermal method. Typically, 52 mmol of citric acid and 52 mmol of glycine were dissolved in 65 mL of deionized water. After complete dissolution, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated at 200 ℃ for 8 h in an oven, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained CDs was stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

FeCl2·4H2O (0.03 mol) and FeCl3·6H2O (0.06 mol) were added into a three-necked flask containing 300 mL deionized water. The solution was then heated to 65 ℃ under N2 atmosphere conditions with constant stirring. Ammonia solution was added slowly into the solution to adjust pH to 11.0. One hour later, the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the black Fe3O4 nanoparticles were obtained. The products were then washed with deionized water until reaching a neutral pH, followed by drying under vacuum at 60 ℃ overnight.

Firstly, 1.5 g of Fe3O4 were dispersed in a mixture solution of isopropanol (250 mL) and deionized water (20 mL) using an ultrasonic oscillator. Thirty minutes later, 25 mL of ammonia solution were added into the mixture under stirring. TEOS (10 mL) were then added and the mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The products Fe3O4@SiO2 were collected using an external magnetic field and then rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, and dried in vacuum at 60 ℃ overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic fluorescent MIPs probe (MFMP)
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4@SiO2 (0.8 g) were dispersed in a 100 mL round bottom flask containing 10 mL of acetonitrile. After ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min, N-Z-L-HSL (1 mmol), MAA (2.5 mmol), HEMA (2.5 mmol) and CDs (10 mL) were added in the mixture with mechanical stirring for 3 h. Then, γ-MAPS (3 mmol), EDGMA (20 mmol) and AIBN (150 mg) were added, and stirred for 1 h. After removal of the dissolved oxygen in the solution by blowing nitrogen for 5 min, the flask was sealed and polymerization was carried out in an oil bath at 75 °C for 24 h. The obtained MFMP was then placed in an oven at 100 °C for aging. The template N-Z-L-HSL was extracted with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) and methanol using Soxhlet extraction method until no N-Z-L-HSL was determined by HPLC (Agilent, USA). Finally, the resultant MFMP were dried at 60 ℃.

For a comparison, magnetic fluorescence non-imprinted polymers probe (MFNP) were prepared using the same polymerization process without the addition of template N-Z-L-HSL and treated in an identical manner.

To evaluate the adsorption kinetics of N-Z-L-HSL onto the MFMP, 20 mg of the fluorescence probe and 4 mL of N-Z-L-HSL methanol solution (2.5 mg/L) were mixed in 50 mL centrifuge tubes, shaking 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40 and 60 min at room temperature, respectively. After separation with the aid of a magnet, the template concentration in the supernatant was analyzed by HPLC (Agilent, USA) with an ultraviolet detector at 210 nm (mobile phase: water/methanol, 3:7, v/v).

The adsorption capacity was calculated basing on the difference of template concentration before and after adsorption using the Eq. (1)
(1)
Q =
C 0
-
C 1
V
m
where Q is adsorption capacity of the MFMP (mg/g), C 0 is the initial concentration of N-Z-L-HSL solution (mg/L), C 1 is the concentration of N-Z-L-HSL in the supernatant after adsorption (mg/L), V is the volume of the initial N-Z-L-HSL solution (mL), and m is the mass of MFMP (g).

The adsorption capacity of the MFMP and MFNP were assessed by static adsorption studies. 20 mg of the magnetic fluorescence probes were incubated with different initial concentrations of N-Z-L-HSL methanol solution (2.5, 5, 10, 15, 30 and 50 mg/L) for 10 h at room temperature. After removal of the saturated probes using a magnet, the concentration of the template in the filtered supernatant was determined by HPLC (Agilent, USA) with an ultraviolet detector at 210 nm (mobile phase: water/methanol, 3:7, v/v). The adsorption capacity was calculated based on the Eq. (1).

20 mg of MFMP were incubated with different concentrations of N-Z-L-HSL, AHLs (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL or C14-HSL) methanol solution or sample extracts in cuvettes for 20 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was recorded in the wavelength range of 380–650 nm upon the excitation at 360 nm with slit widths of 10 nm. The experiment was carried out three replicates and the average data was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of AHLs from bacterial culture medium
ce:section.ce:para: Extraction of AHLs from bacterial culture medium was carried out according to the work with some modification (de Dieu Habimana et al., 2018). All stored bacterial strains of Aeromonas sobria, Aeromonas hydrophila, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were revived on Luria-Bertani (LB) nutrient agar medium at 28 °C for 48 h. Individual colonies were cultured in LB broth at 28 °C to prepare stock cultures of each strain. 1 mL of the stock cultures was cultured in 100 mL of LB broth to produce AHLs signaling molecules. Bacterial growth was monitored by plate counting method and the number of colony-forming units per milliliter (cfu/mL) was obtained. For extraction of the AHLs, 50 mL of culture medium were centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and then the supernatants were filtered through 0.22 μm sterile filter to remove all cell debris. The extraction was carried out three times by adding an equal volume of ethyl acetate containing 0.1% acetic acid (v/v) in the supernatants. The ethyl acetate fractions were collected together and dried under nitrogen with the aid of rotary evaporation. The residues were dissolved in 5 mL methanol and stored at −20 °C for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of food samples and extraction of AHLs
ce:section.ce:para: Published works (Dalgaard, 1995; Jiang et al., 2018) were referred to prepare fish juice sample. Briefly, 1000 g of newly killed fish meat was minced. After adding 500 mL of water, the mixture was uniformly mixed, and boiled for 10 min. The fish juice was obtained by centrifugation and filtration. A mixed solution of 0.1% acetic acid-ethyl acetate (v/v) (100 mL) was added into an equal volume of fish juice spiked with AHLs (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL or C14-HSL) at three different levels (5, 10 and 20 μg/L). After incubation at 25 °C for 2 h with shaking at 180 rpm, the ethyl acetate layer was collected and extraction procedure was repeated three times. The combined ethyl acetate was evaporated under nitrogen with the aid of rotary evaporation. The residues were dissolved in 5 mL methanol and stored at −20 °C for further analysis.

Skim milk (5 mL) spiked with AHLs (C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL or C14-HSL) at three different levels (5, 10 and 20 μg/L) was mixed with 10 mmol/L ammonium acetate (5 mL) to remove protein. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was added 5 mL ethyl acetate containing 0.1% acetic acid (v/v). After incubation at 25 °C for 2 h with shaking at 180 rpm, the ethyl acetate layer was collected and extraction procedure was repeated three times. The combined ethyl acetate was evaporated under nitrogen with the aid of rotary evaporation. The residues were dissolved in 5 mL methanol and stored at −20 °C for further analysis.

The magnetic fluorescence probe was prepared by coating CDs-doped MIPs on the surface of silica-modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The synthesis process of the MFMP was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Modification of Fe3O4 with silica introduced abundant surface hydroxyl groups that can binding the template through hydrogen bonding. CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and glycine as carbon precursors, and embedded in the MIPs layers during polymerization. N-Z-L-HSL molecule owning the same chemical group, five-membered homoserine lactone, with the targets was used firstly as the template for fabrication of AHLs MFMP in this study, employing MAA and HEMA as functional monomers. After removal of the template, the MFMP can rebind the target AHLs molecules due to the formation of three-dimensional cavities complementary to the five-membered homoserine lactone.

FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, CDs and MFMP are shown in Fig. 1 A. For Fe3O4, peak at ~584 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of Fe-O bond. For Fe3O4@SiO2, peak at ~584 cm−1 corresponds to the Fe-O bond while peaks at ~1097 cm−1, ~948 cm−1 and 3443 cm−1 correspond to Si-O-Si, Si-OH and OH groups respectively. The band of CO at ~1717 cm−1, the band of CN at ~1430 cm−1 and the band of OH at ~3450 cm−1 are found in CDs spectrum, indicating the presence of carboxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of CDs. For MFMP, the peak around 584 cm−1 is oriented from Fe-O bond. The bands around ~1728 cm−1, ~1102 cm−1 and ~962 cm−1 could be assigned to the CO, Si-O-Si and Si-OH, respectively, indicating CDs-doped MIPs was successfully synthesized on the surface of silica-modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

The magnetic property of MFMP was assessed by a vibrating sample magnetometer at 298 K. As shown in Fig. 1B, no magnetic hysteresis loops are observed in magnetizaiton curves of Fe3O4 and MFMP, indicating they are superparamagnetic with a magnetical response to an external magnetic field but without magnetization (Tang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2011). Compared with the saturation magnetization (S) of Fe3O4 (66.12 emu/g), the S value of MFMP dropped significantly (3.25 emu/g) due to the shielding effect of the polymers. This resulted in convenient application of the MFMP under common magnetic stirring with good suspensibility, but they could be separated rapidly from samples by a strong magnet.

The XRD spectra of SiO2, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and MFMP are given in Fig. 1C. Six characteristic diffraction peaks (220, 311, 400, 422, 511 and 440) for Fe3O4 in the samples are observed in 2θ range of 10–80°, matching well to the magnetite data in the database of JCPDS (JCPDS card: 19-0629) (Liu et al., 2016). These results indicate no variation for Fe3O4 crystal structure during the fabrication process of magnetic fluorescence probe.

The morphologies of CDs, Fe3O4 and MFMP were studied by TEM. As can be seen in Fig. 1D and E), the average diameter of CDs and Fe3O4 are about 5 nm and 15 nm, respectively. The core–shell structure can be clearly distinguished in the image of MFMP (Fig. 1F), indicating the magnetic probe was fabricated successfully.

The static adsorption study was carried out at the concentrations of N-Z-L-HSL ranging from 0 to 50 mg/L. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the adsorption capacities of MFMP and MFNP increase along with increasing initial concentrations, but the larger equilibrium adsorption amounts of MFMP towards N-Z-L-HSL are always observed at the same initial concentration. When the concentration is 50 mg/L, the amount of N-Z-L-HSL adsorbed to the MFMP (4.9 mg/g) is more than 2.3 times than that of MFNP (2.1 mg/g). The enhanced adsorption mainly owes to the specific affinity adsorption sites in the CDs-doped MIPs on the surface of silica-modified Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

To investigate binding rate of MFMP to the template N-Z-L-HSL, the adsorption capacity of the fluorescence probe was determined at different incubation time. As shown in Fig. 2B, the adsorption of the probe increases rapidly at the beginning and reaches the adsorption equilibrium within 20 min. This rapid mass transfer rate resulted from the surface imprinted MIP on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2, benefiting the development of fast sensing method.

Pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Eq. (2)),
(2)
t
Q t
=
1
k 2
Q
e
2
+
t
Q e
where Qt (mg/g) and Qe (mg/g) are the adsorption capacity at experimental time and at equilibrium, respectively, k 2 (mg/g·min) is the adsorption rate constant, was used to describe the adsorption process. The fitting curve is obtained by t/Qt versus t with correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9939 (Fig. 2B). The Qe (0.62 mg/g) obtained from the experiment was closed to the Qe (0.65 mg/g) calculated from the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, indicating that the rate controlling step, when the probe contact with the template, may be chemical adsorption (Xu, Zhou, Bian, Huang, & Wu, 2011).

As illustrated in Fig. S1, the characteristic UV peak of CDs is a broad adsorption bond centered at 338 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transition of CC gropes and n-π* transition of CO, NH2 and COOH groups. The excitation wavelength-depended fluorescent spectra of CDs are showed in Fig. S2, indicating that the fluorescence intensity is the maximum as the excitation at 370 nm. The fluorescence spectra of MFMP were also studied upon different excitation wavelength. The maximum fluorescence signal of MFMP is observed when the excitation is at 360 nm (Fig. S3). This phenomenon may due to the changing of the size of CDs during the polymerization. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 360 nm was chosen for all experiments.

Fig. S4 presents the fluorescence spectra of MFMP with and without the template. The fluorescence intensity of MFMP with N-Z-L-HSL is about 55% of the MFNP, but remarkable restoration of fluorescence intensity is observed after removal of the template. These results implied the facile and effective removal of N-Z-L-HSL from MFMP. The effect of incubation time between MFMP and N-Z-L-HSL on the fluorescence intensity was assessed. The results (Fig. S5) show the fluorescence intensity decrease with time up to 20 min and then remain stable. Therefore, incubation time of 20 min was chosen for the further experiments.

To demonstrate the capability of recognizing AHLs of MFMP, the probe was incubated with six AHLs (i.e., C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL), N-Z-L-HSL and two structural analogs (metacrate and pirimicarb) at the same concentration. As shown in the Fig. S6, the template and AHLs signaling molecules can remarkably quench the fluorescence of MFMP, but slight effects on the MFNP. The two structural analogs had little influence on the fluorescence signals of MFMP. The results reveal that MFMP has higher sensitive and stronger binding capacity than that of MFNP due to the specific recognition sites for the chemical group of five-membered homoserine lactones. These suggest that the AHLs probe was fabricated successfully using N-Z-L-HSL as an analogue template for multiplex analysis of AHLs signaling molecules.

The fluorescence spectra of MFMP after incubating with different concentration of C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL were recorded. Results in Fig. 3 show that the fluorescence intensity decreases along with increase of concentrations of AHLs.

The fluorescence quenching of MFMP towards the template N-Z-L-HSL, C4-HSL, C6-HSL, C8-HSL, C10-HSL, C12-HSL and C14-HSL signaling molecules were analyzed quantitatively by Stern-Volmer equation (F 0/F = 1 + KsvCq , where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the MFMP before and after adding quencher (analyte), respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant of the quencher, and Cq is the concentration of analyte). There are good linear relationships between fluorescence response (F 0/F) and each AHLs concentrations. The regression equation, linear range, LOD (limit of detection, 3 σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements, and m is the slope of the calibration line) and RSD (relative standard deviations) are listed in Table 1 , showing wide linear range, high sensitivity and good precision for AHLs detection.

It should be noted that there is no spectral overlap between CDs and AHLs and no obvious fluorescence quenching was found as mixing CDs and AHLs in our study. Therefore, fluorescence resonance energy transfer is not considered as possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching. During the polymerization process, some chemical interactions between CDs and monomer, cross-linker, the template and (or) the magnetic core might occur, resulting in the change of fluorophores in the MFMP. When the AHLs were captured by hydrogen bonds, the emitted fluorescence from the MFMP may cause charge transfer from the lone pair of electrons to the hydrogen bonds, leading to the drastic fluorescence quenching behavior. This mechanism of the fluorescence quenching based on photo-induced electron transfer was widely accepted in previous studies (Wang, He, Ji, & Yan, 2009; Liu, Ding, Zhang, & Ding, 2020).
Compared with some methods for analysis of AHLs (Table S1), this fluorescence probe exhibited more advantages such as simplicity, wide linear range, low detection limit and (or) sensitivity.

Stability is a significant factor to estimate the performance of the fluorescence probe. The fluorescence intensity of MFMP was recorded during the storage (10 weeks, 4 °C). The results (Fig. S7) illustrate that the MFMP can be stored for at least 10 weeks at 4 °C without a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the stability of MFMP is good and suitable for sensing AHL in samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AHLs in bacterial suspensions
ce:section.ce:para: Five species of bacteria (i.e., Aeromonas sobria, Aeromonas hydrophila, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus) were cultured to produce AHLs signaling molecules. Because a variety of AHLs molecules were found in the same bacterial (Bruhn et al., 2004), the quantification of AHLs in the bacterial suspensions was determined according to the Stern-Volmer equation for the template N-Z-L-HSL. As shown in Fig. S8, AHLs signaling molecules are sensed in Aeromonas sobria, Aeromonas hydrophila and Pseudomonas aeruginosa suspensions, but no AHLs in Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus suspensions. This is because these two bacteria don't produce AHLs (de Dieu Habimana et al., 2018; Ahmer, 2004). Moreover, these bacterial suspensions were also analyzed by LC–MS according to the paper (Patel et al., 2016). No AHLs was found in Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus suspensions. Linear regression relationships of the MFMP-based fluorescence method and LC–MS data was shown in Fig. S9, and good correlations were obtained. These results indicate the MFMP owned the capability for selective quantification of AHLs in different Gram-negative bacterial supernatants.

Milk and fish products are perishable and thus susceptible to spoilage by bacteria. A large number of bacterial species including Aeromonas hydrophila, Aeromonas sobria, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus had been isolated from the milk and (or) fish (Bruhn et al., 2005; Dar, Dar, Kamili, Chishti, & Ahmad, 2016; Picoli et al., 2017). Therefore, milk and Fish juice samples were selected to evaluate the analytical performance of the developed MFMP by the spiking and recovery tests. Each spiked sample was evaluated at least in pentaplicate. As listed in Table 2 , average recoveries of the analyzed samples are 83.10%–90.74%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are less than 5.1%. The accuracy of the MFMP was validated by comparative determination of the spiked samples using LC–MS according to the paper (Patel et al., 2016). The results are shown in Table 2, indicating that no significant difference between the two methods is observed. Therefore, the proposed MFMP-based fluorescence assay is a simple analytical method with great potential for AHLs sensing in food samples.

Intra-and inter-day assay were carried out to estimate the stability of the MFMP-based fluorescence assay. Intra-day assay was made by analyzing six aliquots of spiked milk and fish juice samples in one day. Inter-day assay was performed on seven consecutive days with the spiked milk and fish juice samples. As shown in Table S2, RSD values of intra-and inter-day tests for the spiked samples ranged from 2.31% to 10.08%, indicating the stability of the developed fluorescent assay is acceptable.

In summary, a multifunctional magnetic fluorescence probe was fabricated by coating CDs-doped MIPs layers on the surface of Fe3O4 particles. N-Z-L-HSL molecular is an excellent analogue template to develop MIPs recognition layers for multiplex recognition of AHLs signaling molecules. The prepared MFMP with fast response and good selectivity could be used to sense AHLs in milk and fish juice samples. This MFMP has great potential to monitor AHLs in agri-food products for early detection of food contamination and prevention of illness caused by food spoilage.

Zhimeng Cui: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Zhuanying Li: Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yuting Jin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Taotao Ren: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jin’ai Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xianghong Wang: Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Keli Zhong: Methodology, Data curation. Lijun Tang: Methodology, Data curation. Yiwei Tang: Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization, Conceptualization. Meirong Cao: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2019YFD0901605) and the Scientific Research Foundation for the Introduced Talent of Hebei Agricultural University (No. YJ201911).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
